claim: Global Mangrove Watch estimates Southeast Asia lost over 30 percent of mangrove cover since 1980, with Indonesia, Myanmar, and Thailand accounting for the largest absolute losses and shrimp-aquaculture expansion as the primary driver, using Landsat and Sentinel-2 imagery at 25m resolution from 1996 to present.
method: Reviewed GMW methodology documentation for detection algorithm, spatial resolution, temporal coverage, mangrove-extent classification, and validation studies. Cross-referenced with regional loss statistics.
limitations: The 25m resolution may miss narrow fringing mangroves less than one pixel wide that nonetheless provide substantial local wave attenuation and nursery habitat.
Pre-1996 mangrove extent must be estimated from coarser-resolution sources, introducing uncertainty in the 1980 baseline.
Aquaculture-attribution relies on concurrent land-use classification that has its own error rates — not all mangrove loss is aquaculture conversion.
Cloud cover in tropical Southeast Asia creates temporal gaps in annual composites, potentially missing short-term deforestation events.

claim: Mangroves reduce storm-surge height by 30-70 percent depending on mangrove width, tree density, species composition, and bathymetry, with wave-energy attenuation of 50-90 percent documented within the first 100-500 meters of mangrove forest.

source.title: The effectiveness of mangroves in reducing coastal flooding

method: Reviewed storm-surge attenuation field studies, hydrodynamic modeling methodology, and mangrove-width-to-attenuation relationship analysis.
limitations: The 30-70 percent range depends heavily on mangrove width, density, and species — applying it to specific sites requires local mangrove-structure data that is rarely available.
Storm-surge attenuation models require bathymetric data that has variable quality in Southeast Asian coastal waters.
The relationship between mangrove width and attenuation is non-linear — very narrow mangrove strips (< 100m) provide minimal protection.
Most field studies are from a limited number of sites; the attenuation relationship may not transfer to all Southeast Asian coastal geomorphologies.

claim: A 2020 Scientific Reports study by Menendez et al. valued global mangrove flood-protection services at $65 billion annually in avoided damages, with Southeast Asia accounting for the largest share due to high coastal population density and cyclone frequency.

source.title: The global flood protection benefits of mangroves

method: Reviewed ecosystem-service valuation methodology, flood-protection modeling, damage-function assumptions, and regional breakdown. Focused on Southeast Asian share and sub-national applicability.
limitations: The $65 billion figure is a global aggregate — sub-national protection values vary enormously based on local population density, infrastructure value, and storm frequency.
Valuation depends on damage functions that assume certain building types and economic exposure — these may not reflect informal coastal settlements common in Southeast Asia.
Protection values are probabilistic — they represent expected annual avoided damages, not actual damage in any specific storm event.
The study does not provide site-level protection values suitable for specific coastal-community planning.

claim: Mangrove deforestation rates have slowed in Thailand and Vietnam due to restoration programs, but accelerated in Indonesia and Myanmar, with annual loss rates of 0.2-0.5 percent of remaining area per year in the highest-pressure regions.

source.title: Drivers and impacts of mangrove deforestation in Southeast Asia

method: Reviewed country-level deforestation rate analysis, driver classification, and restoration program effectiveness assessment for Southeast Asian countries.
limitations: Deforestation rate estimates depend on the satellite product and time window used — different GMW versions and time periods give different rates.
Restoration program effectiveness is difficult to assess — planted mangroves may not provide equivalent ecosystem services to natural mangroves for decades.
Driver classification at deforestation sites requires ground verification that is sparse in Myanmar and eastern Indonesia.
Annual loss rates of 0.2-0.5 percent are aggregate figures for high-pressure regions — individual deforestation events may be much larger at specific sites.
